turns.content: I started using Anki but I'm drowning in reviews. Am I making cards wrong?

Most likely you’re doing a few things that make the workload explode. The good news is you can usually fix it without starting over. Let’s diagnose it quickly: 1) How many new cards per day are you adding (or letting Anki introduce)? 2) Roughly how many reviews are you getting per day right now? 3) Are your cards mostly “front = big prompt, back = big paragraph” or are they short? 4) When you fail a card, do you hit Again a lot (multiple times per session), or do you often “Hard” it? 5) Do you use multiple decks with different settings, or one main deck?

I turned on 30 new cards a day because I wanted to make progress. Now it’s like 300+ reviews daily and I can’t keep up. Cards are mostly question on front, explanation on back, sometimes a list. I’m hitting Again a lot because I can’t remember the exact wording.

That pattern is extremely common: high new-card intake + cards that require exact phrasing = review avalanche. Here’s what’s happening mechanically: - 30 new/day is not insane by itself, but if your retention is low (lots of Again), each new card generates multiple short-interval reviews (minutes, days) and then keeps coming back frequently because the algorithm is trying to stabilize it. - “Exact wording” is a card design problem. Anki is good at strengthening memory of discrete cues/answers, not at forcing verbatim recall of long explanations. If your cards demand precision, you’ll fail them often, which creates more reviews. Immediate triage (do this today): 1) Set New cards/day to 0 for a week. You’re not “falling behind”; you’re digging out. 2) Use a review cap only if you must for mental health (like 200/day), but understand it postpones debt. Better is to pause new cards and complete as many reviews as you can. 3) For the cards you’re failing due to wording, change your grading: if you recalled the idea but not phrasing, press Good (or Hard) and then edit the card later to remove the wording trap. Don’t reinforce perfectionism with Again. Then, redesign: - Convert list-heavy cards into multiple small cards (minimum information principle). - Use cloze deletions or atomic Q/A. - Add constraints: “Give 3 reasons” is better than “Explain everything.” If you answer: do you currently use default intervals/steps? And do you know your “Again” rate (approx)?
I didn’t touch settings. I just use the default. I don’t know my Again rate but it feels like half the time. I also made a lot of cloze cards where I blank multiple things in one sentence.

Default settings are fine for many people, but if you’re failing ~50%, the workload will skyrocket. Two big levers: 1) Card quality (most important). 2) New card rate (second). About cloze with multiple blanks: that’s a classic workload trap. A single cloze note with many deletions can behave like many cards. Also, if you blank multiple items in the same sentence, you’re testing a big chunk at once; if you miss one, you fail the whole card. Fix cloze usage: - Prefer one concept per cloze card. If you need multiple blanks, consider separate cloze cards (Cloze 1, Cloze 2…) but keep each deletion testing one fact. - Avoid “enumeration clozes” where you blank a list of 6 items. Instead: make 6 cards, each asking for one item, or ask for “name any 2/3” with a different format (but Anki can’t easily grade “any 2” so you’d need careful design). Practical plan for the next 7 days: Day 1–2: Stop new cards. Just reviews. While reviewing, tag bad cards (e.g., tag “needs_edit”). Day 3–7: Each day, spend 20–30 minutes editing a batch of tagged cards: - Split long answers. - Remove “exact wording” requirements. - Add images/structure if helpful (but not huge paragraphs). - Add “extra” field for explanations; keep the answer field short. Also, consider adjusting learning steps: Default steps are often 1m 10m. If you’re hitting Again repeatedly within the same session, it can feel brutal. You can use something like 10m 1d (or 15m 1d) so you don’t churn the same card 6 times in one sitting. But that’s optional; don’t tweak settings until card design and new-card rate are under control. Do you mostly review on desktop or phone? And do you use the “Again/Hard/Good/Easy” buttons thoughtfully, or mostly Again/Good?
Mostly on my phone. I usually do Again or Good. Hard feels like it doesn’t do anything. Easy I almost never press because I’m scared it’ll disappear for months.

Phone-only usage tends to push people toward quick taps and less editing, which can worsen card quality over time. Also, your grading habits matter a lot. How the buttons “should” feel: - Again = you failed to retrieve the answer. Use it when you truly didn’t know, not when you were imperfect. - Hard = you got it but it was slow/effortful or shaky. - Good = you got it with reasonable effort. - Easy = you got it quickly and confidently, and you’d likely get it right tomorrow too. Your fear about Easy is understandable, but if you never press Easy, you inflate workload because cards don’t get longer intervals when they deserve it. That said, if your cards are poorly calibrated, Easy can be risky. So we fix the calibration first. A practical grading rule (helps a lot): - If you recalled the core answer without looking: Good (even if you didn’t say it elegantly). - If you recalled after a long struggle but still before revealing: Hard. - If you had to reveal to know: Again. - If it was instantly obvious: Easy. On phone, one workflow improvement: “flag” or “mark” cards that are badly phrased. Don’t try to rewrite them on the phone mid-session. Then do a weekly desktop editing session. Now, to tackle “Hard feels like it doesn’t do anything”: depending on settings, Hard often gives a smaller interval increase than Good, but it still matters. It’s your “I knew it but it’s not stable” signal. If you replace many Again taps with Hard, you’ll reduce relearning churn. Would you be willing to share an example of one of your typical cards (front/back text, anonymized)? I can show how to rewrite it to cut review load.
Example: Front: “What are the main points of [concept]?” Back: Then I wrote like 8 bullet points with explanations. I feel like I need to remember all 8.

That card is almost guaranteed to create pain. It’s too broad (“main points” is vague), too many items (8), and the grading is all-or-nothing. Rewrite strategy: split, specify, and reduce required output. Option A: Split into 8 atomic cards Each card tests one bullet point: Front: “In [concept], what is one key point about X?” Back: “X is … (1–2 lines). Extra: longer explanation.” Option B: Use prompts that constrain output Instead of “main points,” choose: - “What is the single most important idea of [concept]?” - “What problem does [concept] solve?” - “What are the 2 common pitfalls of [concept]?” - “What is the difference between [concept] and [related concept]?” This yields fewer cards with higher signal. Option C: Hierarchical cards (good for frameworks) 1) One overview card: “Name the 3 categories of [concept].” 2) Then subcards for each category: “Within category A, what are the 2 key items?” 3) Then detail cards for each item. This preserves structure while keeping each retrieval manageable. Also: if you truly need 8 bullets, “remember all 8” is a different skill than spaced repetition alone. Anki can help, but it’s best done with a set of cards that individually test each bullet, plus occasional synthesis practice outside Anki (like writing a short summary from memory once a week). Don’t force synthesis into every daily review. If you have a lot of these “main points” cards, that’s your review tsunami. How many cards are in your deck total, and how many are these broad-summary style cards?
Total about 2000 cards. A lot are broad like that. Maybe 30-40%? I just thought that was the point: learn the whole concept.

2000 cards with 30–40% being broad “concept dump” cards will absolutely overwhelm you. The point of Anki isn’t “store entire concepts in one card”; it’s to schedule retrieval practice efficiently. That only works if the retrieval unit is small enough to succeed most of the time. A realistic target: mature-card accuracy around 85–95%. If you’re failing half your reps, the system is yelling: “cards are too hard or too many new.” A staged rescue plan (so you don’t have to nuke everything): Stage 1 — Stop the bleeding (1 week) - New/day = 0. - Reviews: do as many as you can without burning out. Aim for consistency. - Tag broad cards as “split_me”. Stage 2 — Convert the worst offenders (2–3 weeks) Each day convert, say, 10–30 broad cards into atomic ones. You’ll likely increase card count, but paradoxically reduce total time because each review becomes quick and passable. Rule: if the answer takes longer than ~10–15 seconds to say, it’s too big. Stage 3 — Controlled intake (ongoing) Reintroduce new cards at a rate you can sustain. Often this is 5–15/day depending on time. You can ramp up once your retention stabilizes. Important: Splitting increases card count, but decreases failure rate and per-card time. Daily reviews often drop. Do you have a time budget you can realistically spend per day on Anki? Like 15 min, 30 min, 1 hour?
I can do 30 minutes most days. Sometimes 45. Right now it takes me over an hour and I still don’t finish.

With a 30–45 minute budget, you need to run Anki like a production system: stable throughput, controlled input, continuous quality improvement. Concrete numbers: - Many people average ~6–12 seconds per review on well-made cards. - 30 minutes = 1800 seconds. - At 10 seconds/review, that’s ~180 reviews/day. So your sustainable review load is probably ~150–250 reviews/day depending on card quality and focus. You’re at 300+ reviews/day with high failure, so you’re exceeding capacity. Actionable settings and habits (aligned to your time budget): 1) New cards/day Set to 0 until your daily reviews drop under ~200 and you can clear them in 30–45 minutes. 2) Learning steps (optional but helpful on phone) If you find the “Again -> comes back in 1 minute -> fail again” loop demoralizing, change steps to something like: - Steps (in minutes): 15 1440 Meaning: after you learn it, you see it again in 15 minutes, then next day. This reduces same-session churn. Downside: slightly more forgetting next day, but overall less torture. 3) Lapses Default lapse steps can also cause churn. Keep it simple: if you forget a mature card, it should come back soon, but not necessarily 5 times in the same sitting. 4) Card design rule-of-thumb - One question, one answer. - Answer: ideally 1 line; explanation goes in “extra.” - Avoid “What are the main points…” unless you specify a small number and you truly want enumeration practice. 5) Review strategy - Don’t aim for perfection. Aim for consistent correct retrieval. - If you “kind of know it,” Hard/Good rather than Again, then edit later. If you want, we can define a “card template” you’ll follow when creating new cards so you don’t recreate the problem.
Yes please. I’m honestly confused what a good card looks like. I keep either making them too big or too trivial.

A good card hits a sweet spot: it tests something meaningful but in a small, clearly graded unit. A practical template you can reuse: 1) Cue is specific and unambiguous Bad: “Explain [concept].” Good: “What is the definition of [concept] in one sentence?” Good: “What is the purpose of [concept]?” Good: “What’s the difference between [concept A] and [concept B]?” 2) Answer is short and checkable Aim for: - 1 sentence, or - 1–3 bullet points, or - a single formula/rule. If it needs a paragraph, move the paragraph to Extra. 3) Include context if needed, but keep it stable If you keep forgetting because the cue is too generic, add a hint: - “In the context of X…” - “When doing Y…” - “Given scenario Z…” 4) Use “why” and “when” cards sparingly but intentionally These are higher-level and can be valuable, but they should still be constrained: - “Why does [rule] help? (1–2 reasons)” - “When should you NOT use [tool]? (2 cases)” 5) Avoid multi-target prompts Bad: “What are the causes, symptoms, and treatment of X?” Split into three cards. 6) Add examples as separate cards If you need to recognize or apply, make cards like: Front: “Given [mini example], what does this illustrate?” Back: “It illustrates [principle] because …” A simple “card creation checklist”: - Can I answer in < 10 seconds? - Would I grade myself easily? - If I forgot it, would I know what part I forgot? If any answer is “no,” split or rewrite. Now, about “too trivial”: trivial cards happen when they test something with no decision value. Fix by anchoring to use: - “What’s the key distinction…” - “What’s the consequence if…” - “What’s the minimal rule to decide between A and B…” If you describe one of your “too trivial” cards, I can show how to upgrade it.
Like “What is [term]?” and the answer is just the definition. It feels like rote memorization.

Definitions can be rote, but they’re often useful if they enable later reasoning. The trick is to pair a definition card with one or two “use” cards. A good trio: 1) Definition (compressed) Front: “Define [term] (one sentence).” Back: “…” 2) Discrimination Front: “What’s the difference between [term] and [nearby term]?” Back: “Term = …; Nearby term = …; difference is …” 3) Application/recognition Front: “Is this an example of [term]? [short example]” Back: “Yes/no, because … (1 line).” This way, the definition isn’t floating; it’s connected to decisions. Also, definitions become less rote if you force them into your own words. Put the definition in simple language, not copied text. If you copied a formal definition, you’ll keep failing due to wording. Phone-friendly approach: keep the definition card, but add an “Extra” field with the formal definition if you need it. Your answer field is your paraphrase. Do you currently copy/paste explanations into the back? Or write in your own words?
Mostly copy/paste. It saves time. But yeah then I can’t reproduce it.

Copy/paste is a major cause of “I recognize it but can’t recall it.” It creates cards that look authoritative but aren’t encoded in your own language. A better workflow that’s still fast: 1) Read the source. 2) Close it. 3) Write a 1-sentence answer from memory in plain language. 4) Put the copied paragraph into Extra as reference. This forces initial processing (which is what you’re currently skipping). It’s slower per card but drastically reduces downstream reviews and failures. If time is your concern, batch it: - Capture raw material quickly (copy into a “source notes” document). - Later, convert into Anki cards by paraphrasing. Also, don’t make cards from everything you read. Only from things that: - you want to be able to recall without looking up, and - are likely to matter repeatedly. Now, one more thing: your daily new cards were 30. If those were mostly copy/paste + broad prompts, you created a backlog of low-quality cards at high volume. That’s the perfect storm. Would you prefer to salvage existing cards (editing) or suspend and rebuild gradually?
I don’t want to waste what I already did. But editing 2000 cards sounds impossible.

You don’t need to edit all 2000—just the ones causing disproportionate pain. Usually, 20% of cards generate 80% of the frustration. Use a “triage by failure” method: 1) In Anki’s Browser, sort by “Lapses” or “Ease” (depending on version). Cards with many lapses or very low ease are problematic. 2) Tag those and focus your editing there. 3) Also tag broad-summary cards as you encounter them during reviews. Practical weekly schedule (fits your time budget): - Daily: 30–45 min reviews only (no new cards). - Twice per week: 30 min editing session on desktop/browser: - Find the 30 worst cards (high lapses / low ease). - For each: either split, rewrite, or suspend if it’s not worth it. Suspending is not failure. Some cards are just not good targets for spaced repetition. If something is better learned through practice or reference, suspend it. If you want a metric: any card that repeatedly takes >30 seconds or causes repeated failures is a candidate for redesign or suspension. Do you know how to use the Browser to search and tag? If not, I can walk you through a simple process.
I’ve barely used the browser. I just hit study. How do I even find low ease cards?

On desktop: 1) Open Anki → Browse. 2) On the left, select your deck (or All decks). 3) In the list, add columns if needed (right-click the header row): Ease, Lapses, Interval, Due, etc. 4) Click the “Ease” column to sort ascending (lowest first). Those are cards Anki thinks you struggle with. 5) Or sort by “Lapses” descending to see the most-forgotten cards. Then: - Select a batch (e.g., top 50 worst). - Tag them “triage” (right panel → Tags). - Go through and decide: edit / split / suspend. Useful searches: - prop:lapses>3 (cards lapsed more than 3 times) - prop:ease<2.0 (depending on how Anki displays ease; often 250% is normal, so you might search for ease: or just sort) - is:due (due today) - is:new (new cards) If you’re mostly on phone, you can still do minimal triage by flagging cards during review, then later filter flagged cards on desktop. If you tell me your Anki version (2.1.x?) I can be more exact, but the above is generally accurate.
Okay. Another problem: I keep adding cards because I feel guilty if I don’t. Like I have so much material and I’m falling behind.

That guilt loop is the hidden driver of review overload. You’re treating Anki like a to-do list for “everything you encountered,” but it works best as a curated memory system. A more sustainable mindset: - Your goal is not to “Anki-ify” everything. - Your goal is to maintain a set of high-value memories at high retention with low friction. Think of Anki like a subscription: every new card is a recurring obligation. If you add 30/day, you’re signing up for thousands of future reviews. A rule that helps: Before making a card, ask: “Would I pay 10 seconds every few months for the next 2 years to keep this in memory?” If no, don’t card it. Put it in notes or a searchable document. Also, you can separate “capture” from “commit”: - Capture: put stuff in a temporary inbox deck or a text file. - Commit: only turn a small portion into Anki cards after you’ve decided it’s worth it and you can phrase it well. If you like, we can set a hard “Anki budget”: - Max reviews/day you can sustain. - Max new cards/day you can sustain. Then you commit to never exceeding it. That’s how you prevent guilt from controlling the system. What would be a comfortable daily new-card number once you’re stable? 5? 10? 15?
Maybe 10. But I’m scared even 10 will make it blow up again.

10/day is often sustainable if (a) cards are good and (b) you keep retention reasonable. But you’re right to be cautious. Here’s a safe ramp-up protocol: 1) First stabilize: get daily reviews under control with New=0 for at least 7–14 days. 2) Then start with 5 new/day for a week. 3) If daily reviews remain within your time budget, increase to 7–10/day. 4) If reviews creep up, drop back down immediately. Don’t “power through”; that’s how it snowballs. You can also set a “new cards only after reviews” rule. Anki has an option (or your own discipline) to finish reviews first. That prevents adding more when you’re already behind. Another important lever: retention target. If you’re aiming for near-perfect recall, you’ll over-review. Many people do fine with ~85–90% retention on mature cards. Trying to force 98% can double workload. So: aim for “good enough” memory plus easy lookup for details. Do you often re-read the back of cards and feel like you need to memorize the whole explanation? That can also slow you down.
Yes. I read the back and think “I should remember all this,” and then I feel bad and press Again.

That’s a key behavior to change. In Anki, the back side is often a reference, not a second thing to memorize. Use a strict separation: - Answer field = what you must be able to recall. - Extra field = explanation, nuance, examples, links. When reviewing: 1) Try to recall the answer field. 2) Flip. 3) Grade based on whether you recalled the answer field. 4) Optionally skim Extra only if you’re curious or you got it wrong. If you always read Extra, review time explodes and you reinforce the belief that everything must be memorized. So, concrete step: edit your note type so you have an “Extra” field, and move long explanations there. Then keep “Back” minimal. If you already have long backs, you can still adopt a rule: your grading is based on the first line only. Add a separator like: Back: Answer: … --- Extra: … (longer) That way you don’t punish yourself for not recalling the whole essay. Would you like a suggested note format (fields + card templates) you can implement?
Yes. But I’m not technical. I’m scared I’ll break Anki.

You can keep it simple and safe. You don’t have to do fancy templates. Easiest approach (no template changes): - Just start formatting your existing backs with a consistent structure. For example, every back becomes: Answer (1–2 lines): - … Extra (optional): - Explanation… - Example… - Link… You can do that manually while editing problem cards. No settings changes required. If you do want fields (slightly more technical, but still safe): 1) On desktop: Tools → Manage Note Types. 2) Select your note type (e.g., “Basic” or “Cloze”). 3) Click Fields… → Add → name it “Extra”. 4) Then Cards… → edit the back template to include {{Extra}} under a divider. This won’t delete anything; it just adds a place to put extra info. Your existing cards won’t break; they’ll just have an empty Extra field until you fill it. But manual formatting is enough if you’re anxious. Which do you use more: Basic cards or Cloze cards?
Mostly Cloze because it’s faster to make. I highlight a sentence and blank things.

Cloze is fast, but it’s also easy to misuse. Used well, it’s great. Used as “blank half the paragraph,” it becomes a memory torture device. Rules for effective cloze: 1) One deletion tests one fact. Bad: “{{c1::A}}, {{c2::B}}, {{c3::C}}, {{c4::D}} are the steps…” This creates multiple cards and often depends on the same context; you may memorize the sentence rather than the idea. Better: Make separate notes or rewrite as Q/A cards. 2) Keep enough context visible. If the cloze hides too much, you’re guessing. 3) Avoid “overlapping clozes” that require recalling a list in order. Unless order matters, don’t test order. 4) Use hints in cloze: {{c1::answer::hint}} The hint can reduce ambiguity without giving it away. A good cloze example structure: Text: “The purpose of X is {{c1::to do Y::purpose}}.” Extra: “Longer explanation…” If you want speed plus quality: use cloze for single-sentence facts and use Basic for comparisons, “why,” and application. Also, cloze can hide poor understanding. If you can’t explain it in your own words, cloze will feel like pattern recognition. Do you feel like many cloze cards are just “fill in the blank” without meaning?
Yes. I sometimes can guess the missing word because I remember the sentence, not because I understand.

That’s the “sentence memory” problem. Anki can reinforce surface patterns if the card is too tied to a specific phrasing. To force meaning: - Rewrite the sentence in your own words before clozing. - Or convert to a question that requires retrieval, not pattern completion. Instead of cloze: “The key benefit of X is …” Ask: Front: “What is the key benefit of X?” Back: “It is … (1 sentence).” Or use cloze with paraphrase: Write a short, plain-language statement, then cloze the key term. Also, add an example card: Front: “Which principle is being used here? [example]” Back: “X, because …” That breaks the dependency on a single sentence. A good benchmark: if you can answer the card without seeing the exact original sentence, it’s probably meaningful. Do you have a backlog of new cards not yet learned, or is the problem mainly reviews?
Both. I have like 600 new cards waiting because I stopped doing new ones for a few days. But I keep feeling like I should clear them.

600 new cards waiting is a psychological weight, but you don’t need to clear them quickly. You can treat them as an inbox. Two options: Option 1: Keep them as New but set New/day low (eventually). Option 2: Move them into a separate “Inbox” deck and only pull a few per day after you’ve edited them. I recommend the inbox approach: - Create a deck called “Inbox”. - Move those 600 new cards there. - Then, only move curated cards into your main deck at a steady rate (like 5–10/day). Why this helps: - Your main deck remains stable and predictable. - The inbox can be messy; it doesn’t affect daily workload until you choose. If moving feels like effort, you can also just keep them where they are and set New/day = 0 until you’re ready. But visually, seeing “600 new” can trigger guilt. Also, it’s worth asking: are all 600 worth memorizing? You might suspend a large chunk and keep the information elsewhere. Would you be comfortable suspending cards that aren’t high value, even if you made them?
Suspending feels like throwing away work. But maybe. How do I decide what’s “high value” without knowing the future?

Good question. You can’t predict perfectly, so use heuristics. High-value cards tend to be: - Frequently used ideas/rules (they come up again and again). - Foundational concepts that support many other things. - Things you repeatedly forget and then regret forgetting. - Discriminations that prevent common mistakes (“don’t confuse A with B”). - Procedures you need to execute without looking up. Lower-value cards tend to be: - Rare details you can quickly look up. - Long lists where you only need recognition, not recall. - Cards that are basically copied text with no personal processing. - Anything that feels like trivia for you. A practical decision method: For each questionable card, ask: 1) If I forgot this, would it cause confusion or slow me down significantly? 2) Could I look it up in under 30 seconds when needed? 3) Does this connect to many other cards? If (1) is no and (2) is yes, suspend. If it’s foundational, keep. If you’re unsure, tag it “maybe” and revisit in a month. Also, suspending isn’t deleting. You can always unsuspend later. Think of it as putting it in cold storage. If you want, we can set up three tags: - keep - maybe - suspend_candidate Then you can batch-suspend later once you’re confident.
Okay. Another thing: I sometimes do Anki late at night and I’m exhausted, so I fail everything and it gets worse.

That’s a real effect. Anki assumes your performance reflects memory stability, but fatigue makes you press Again more, which tells Anki “this is unstable,” creating more reviews. Two fixes: 1) Schedule reviews when you’re alert (even 10 minutes earlier in the day helps). 2) Use a “minimum viable review” mode when tired: - If it’s late and you’re exhausted, do only a small fixed number (e.g., 50 reviews) and stop. - Or do only “new learning” when fresh, and reviews later—though generally reviews first is better. Also, adjust how you grade when tired: - If you obviously know it but your brain is slow, use Hard instead of Again. - Reserve Again for true blank-outs. If fatigue is frequent, consider setting a daily “review appointment” earlier. Consistency beats hero sessions. Do you have a typical time of day you could reserve for Anki? Even 20 minutes?
Mornings are hard. Maybe during lunch or right after work. But I also procrastinate because I dread the pile.

Dread comes from unpredictability and negative feedback (endless Again). Once you stabilize the workload and improve card quality, dread often drops. To reduce procrastination now: - Create a “starter ritual”: open Anki, do 10 reviews only. If you want to stop after 10, you can. Usually you’ll continue because starting is the hard part. - Use a timer: 15 minutes. Stop when it rings. This prevents the “I’m trapped” feeling. - Hide the scary numbers: some people avoid looking at total due and just start. And most importantly: stop adding new cards until the pile is manageable. Otherwise you’re feeding the monster you dread. If you want a concrete daily routine: 1) 15–25 minutes: Reviews. 2) 5 minutes: Flag 2–3 painful cards. 3) Twice per week: 30 minutes editing flagged cards. That’s it. No extra guilt tasks.
I like the timer idea. But I still worry that if I don’t finish all due cards every day I’m “breaking” the algorithm.

Not finishing due cards isn’t ideal, but it’s not catastrophic. The algorithm degrades gracefully. The bigger issue is letting a backlog grow for weeks. Think of it like this: - If you’re short one day, you’ll have slightly more tomorrow. - If you’re short every day by a lot, you accumulate debt. So the goal is to match average daily throughput to average incoming workload. If you can’t finish due cards today, the best move is: - Reduce inputs (new cards = 0). - Improve retention (better cards, better grading). - Increase throughput slightly (shorter cards, less reading of Extra). You can also use filtered decks to manage: - Create a filtered deck for “overdue” or “leech” cards. But I’d keep it simple until you’re comfortable. A helpful metric to watch is “reviews per day trend.” If it’s stable or decreasing, you’re fine even if you miss a day occasionally.
What’s a leech card? I saw that word but ignored it.

A leech is a card you repeatedly forget. Anki marks it after a certain number of lapses (defaults vary), because such cards often waste time. Leeches happen because: - The card is poorly written (ambiguous, too much info). - The information isn’t meaningful to you. - You’re missing prerequisite understanding. - It’s a “list with many items” card. What to do with leeches: - Don’t keep brute-forcing them. - Edit: simplify, add context, add a mnemonic, or split into smaller cards. - Or suspend if it’s not worth it. You can configure Anki to tag leeches automatically and optionally suspend them automatically. I recommend auto-tagging but not auto-suspending at first, so you can review them consciously. If you find you have many leeches, that’s a signal your card design needs adjustment.
I think I have a lot. Some cards I’ve failed like 10 times. It’s embarrassing.

It’s not embarrassing; it’s diagnostic. Repeated failure is almost never a willpower issue—it’s a design issue. When you see a chronic fail card, ask: 1) Is the question too broad? 2) Is the answer too long? 3) Is it testing something you don’t actually understand? 4) Is it too similar to another card (interference)? 5) Is the cue too weak (you can’t tell what it’s asking)? Common fixes by symptom: - Broad question → split into multiple cards. - Long answer → reduce answer to 1 line, move rest to Extra. - Missing understanding → go back to source, write a simpler explanation, add an example card. - Interference (two similar items) → make a comparison card explicitly contrasting them, and add unique cues. - Weak cue → add context, or rephrase question to be more specific. Also, sometimes you just need a mnemonic, but use that after you’ve simplified the card. If you can recall the answer when you see it but not from the cue, the cue is wrong.
Interference is huge for me. I mix up similar things and then fail both cards and it spirals.

Interference is a real cognitive phenomenon. Anki can help if you design cards to reduce confusion rather than create it. Anti-interference card patterns: 1) Contrast card Front: “Compare A vs B: what’s the key difference?” Back: “A = …; B = …; difference: …” 2) “Which one?” card (forced choice) Front: “Which one applies: A or B? [short scenario]” Back: “A, because …” 3) Unique cue card If two items share a lot, add a distinctive hook: Front: “What is the distinctive feature of A (not shared with B)?” Back: “A has …” 4) Bidirectional linking (careful) Sometimes you need both “A -> definition” and “definition -> A,” but too many reverse cards can increase interference. Use reverse only when you truly need recall in that direction. Also, avoid making two cards that are nearly identical except one word. That’s interference bait. If you want, you can tag interfering pairs and create one explicit comparison card, then suspend one of the redundant definition cards.
I think I made tons of reverse cards automatically. I checked a box that makes it both ways. Is that bad?

It can be. “Basic (and reversed card)” doubles your workload immediately, and the reverse direction isn’t always useful. Reverse cards are valuable when: - You need to produce the “front” given the “back” in real life. - The mapping is truly bidirectional and not too ambiguous. Reverse cards are risky when: - Many items share similar definitions (reverse becomes guessy). - The “back” isn’t unique (multiple possible fronts). - You don’t actually need to recall in that direction. If you’re overloaded, disabling reverse for most cards is one of the fastest workload reductions. How to fix without deleting: - For note types that generate two cards, you can suspend the reverse card selectively. In the Browser, you’ll see Card 1 and Card 2. You can suspend Card 2 for most notes. A quick win: - Identify a batch of reverse cards. - Suspend the reverse direction unless you’re sure you need it. This alone can cut daily reviews by a large fraction.
That sounds like a lifesaver. But how do I know which direction to keep?

Keep the direction that matches how you’ll use the knowledge. A practical test: - If you’re more likely to see a cue and need to recall the definition/explanation, keep “term → meaning.” - If you’re more likely to encounter a situation/definition and need to name/choose the term, keep “meaning → term.” If you don’t know, default to keeping the direction that is: - less ambiguous - faster to answer - more frequently needed Often “term → meaning” is less ambiguous. Also, consider recognition vs recall: - If in real life you only need to recognize the right term among options, you might not need “meaning → term” recall. You can also keep a few reverse cards for the most important items and suspend the rest. If you want a systematic approach: 1) Sort by “Card” column to isolate reverse cards. 2) Suspend reverse for all. 3) Unsuspend reverse only for items where you truly need that direction.
I’m worried I’ll forget everything if I suspend too much.

Suspending reduces practice, so yes, you’ll retain less of whatever you suspend—but that’s the point if it was low-value or redundant. Your memory budget is limited. Keeping everything means you remember nothing well because you burn out. A sustainable system beats an aspirational one. Also, you’re not choosing between “remember everything” and “remember nothing.” You’re choosing between: - A smaller set you maintain consistently, and - A huge set you abandon. Consistency wins. If it helps emotionally, treat suspension as “archiving.” You’re curating your deck.
Okay. Let’s talk settings. People online have all these settings and add-ons. Should I copy someone’s “optimal” settings?

Don’t copy “optimal” settings blindly. Settings can fine-tune, but they can’t compensate for bad cards or unsustainable new-card rates. That said, a few settings principles can help if you’re overwhelmed: 1) New cards/day: low and controlled (you already know this). 2) Learning steps: reduce same-session churn if it’s killing you. 3) Leech threshold: keep default or slightly lower, and tag leeches. 4) Maximum interval: usually leave it high unless you have a reason. 5) Bury related cards: often helpful to reduce interference (bury siblings so you don’t see near-identical cards the same day). “Bury related new cards” and “Bury related reviews” can reduce the feeling of seeing the same fact in multiple forms immediately, which can create illusions of mastery or interference. Add-ons: avoid until you’re stable. They add complexity. If you tell me your biggest pain point in sessions—endless relearning loops, too many daily due, or too many failures—I can recommend a minimal settings adjustment.
Biggest pain is endless relearning loops. I hit Again and it comes back like 1 minute later and I fail again because I’m annoyed.

Then adjusting learning steps is reasonable. Goal: fewer immediate repeats, more spaced repeats. A simple setting change: - Learning steps: 15m 1d Instead of 1m 10m. Meaning: when you see a new card and miss it, it won’t hammer you every minute; it’ll come back after 15 minutes, and then the next day. For lapses (forgetting a learned card), you can use a similar step: - Lapse steps: 15m (or 10m) And consider “New interval” (the percent of previous interval) around default. Keep it simple. Caution: If you push the steps too far apart, you may forget more and press Again tomorrow, but many people prefer that to same-session torture. With good cards, it works fine. Also: when you’re annoyed, your brain is not encoding well. So reducing annoyance can improve learning. Try this for a week while New/day = 0 and see if your mood and throughput improve.
If I change steps, will it mess up existing cards?

It won’t “mess up” matured cards. Learning steps mainly affect: - New cards you’re learning - Cards you hit Again on (relearning) Existing cards already in longer intervals won’t suddenly reset. But when you fail them, the relearning behavior will follow your new lapse steps. That’s usually fine. Before changing anything, you can export your deck or just note your current settings so you can revert.
Another question: is it better to do reviews in one big session or split across the day?

Splitting often helps, especially on phone: - Short sessions reduce fatigue and mind-wandering. - You can take advantage of natural gaps (waiting, transit, breaks). But there’s a catch: if you adjust learning steps to 15m, splitting can help you catch the 15-minute repeats naturally. If you do one single session, you might not see those repeats until later anyway. A good pattern: - Session 1 (midday): do all due reviews. - Session 2 (evening): mop up remaining reviews + any learning steps that became due. Avoid late-night only sessions if they cause failures. Also, if you’re doing “new cards” eventually, it’s better to do them when you’re freshest, then let the short-step reviews happen later.
I also sometimes change my mind mid-review and think “I knew it” after I already hit Again.

That happens. The important thing is to set a consistent grading rule to reduce second-guessing. A simple rule: - Grade based on whether you could produce the answer before flipping. Not “would I have gotten it eventually,” not “it feels familiar,” but “did I retrieve it?” If you hit Again and then regret it, don’t dwell. But if it’s frequent, it may mean your cards are too strict (wording) or too ambiguous. You can also use the “Undo” feature right away if you truly mis-tapped, but don’t use it to negotiate with yourself. Better: make the card easier to grade (shorter answer, clearer prompt), so you’re not stuck in gray areas.
How do I handle cards where the answer is like 5 items? I can remember 3 but not all 5.

That’s a classic “partial recall” grading problem. For Anki, you want cards where success/failure is clear. Best approach: split into 5 cards. Each card asks for one item, or uses a cue that makes each item unique. If you truly need all 5 together (because they function as a set), you can do: - One “set” card: “List the 5 items” (for occasional synthesis) - Plus 5 individual cards (daily maintenance) Then, when you miss 2 of 5, you don’t fail the whole set every time; you learn the missing ones individually. If you refuse to split, you can change the prompt: - “Name at least 3 of the 5 items.” But that makes grading subjective and can lead to holes. Splitting is cleaner.
Splitting everything seems like it will create 10,000 cards. That sounds worse.

It sounds worse, but it usually isn’t. Card count is not the enemy; daily time is. One big card that takes 60 seconds and you fail often is worse than 6 small cards that take 8 seconds each and you pass. Also, splitting doesn’t necessarily multiply total information; it just distributes it into manageable retrieval units. That said, don’t split mindlessly. Use structure: - If items are truly independent, split. - If they’re best understood as a concept, keep a concept card plus a few key details. And remember: you don’t need to Anki every detail. Your deck can be smaller by being more selective, even if you split some cards.
What about “image occlusion” type cards? People say they’re great.

They can be great when visual recall matters and the labels are meaningful. But they can also become rote “label memorization” with low transfer. If you use them: - Keep each occlusion card focused (one label at a time). - Add a second card that asks for the function/meaning, not just the name. - Avoid occluding 30 labels on one image if you don’t need all of them. Also, image cards can be slower to review on phone and can increase time per review. With your time budget, be cautious. If you’re already drowning, I wouldn’t introduce a new card type until your core workflow is stable.
I feel like I’m constantly tinkering. I want a stable system.

Then adopt a “minimal viable system” and freeze it for a month. Minimal viable system: - One main deck. - One inbox deck. - New cards/day: 0 until stable; then 5–10. - Review daily with a timer. - Edit only flagged/triage cards twice per week. - No new add-ons, no major settings changes. And a “definition of done” for each day: - Do 30 minutes (or clear due if it takes less). That’s it. Not “finish everything forever,” just hit your daily quota consistently. Stability comes from routine, not perfect settings.
Can you help me with an inbox workflow? I like capturing stuff but it’s too much.

Yes. Here’s a simple inbox workflow that prevents overload: 1) Capture phase (fast) When you encounter something you might want to remember: - Put it into a single “Inbox” note as raw text, or - Make a crude Anki note in the Inbox deck with the source text in Extra, but leave the “Answer” blank or rough. Key: don’t polish during capture. 2) Processing phase (scheduled) Twice per week, 30 minutes: - Open Inbox deck. - For each item: a) Decide: keep/suspend. b) If keep: rewrite into 1–3 good cards (paraphrased, atomic). c) Move to Main deck. 3) Intake control Only move a fixed number per day/week into Main. Example: move 50/week if you can sustain 7/day. 4) “Aging out” If an inbox item sits for 30 days unprocessed, it’s probably not important. Suspend/archive it. This mirrors productivity systems: capture everything, but only commit selectively.
That helps. But I’m not sure how to rewrite quickly. I stare at the raw text and blank.

Use a small set of rewrite prompts. When processing an inbox item, choose 1–2 of these card types: A) Definition “Define X in one sentence.” B) Purpose “What is X for?” C) Steps “What are the 3 steps of X?” (only if it’s truly 3 and stable) Or split each step. D) Difference “What’s the difference between X and Y?” E) Trigger/when “When would you use X?” / “When not?” F) Example classification “Given this example, is it X? Why?” If you can’t fit the item into one of these, it might be too vague or not suitable for Anki yet. Put it in notes, not flashcards. Also: allow yourself to create fewer cards. One high-quality card beats five mediocre ones.
Sometimes I feel like I need to capture the nuance, otherwise I’m oversimplifying.

Nuance is important, but Anki is not great at storing nuance as “daily recall.” It’s better at core distinctions and triggers. Strategy for nuance: - Put nuance in Extra field. - Create one “boundary” card if it matters: “What’s an exception to X?” or “What’s a common misconception about X?” But keep it limited. If you create five nuance cards for every concept, your workload explodes. Most nuance can remain as reference that you revisit when needed. A helpful principle: store what you need to retrieve quickly; store nuance where you can look it up.
Okay but I still worry I’m using Anki “wrong” because people say you should never press Hard or something.

There’s a lot of dogma online. In practice: - The buttons exist because your recall isn’t binary. - Using Hard appropriately can improve scheduling and reduce unnecessary lapses. - The bigger risk is misusing Again for “not perfect,” which you’re doing. So ignore the “never press Hard” advice. Use the buttons to reflect your experience honestly. If you want consistency: - Use Again only for true failure. - Use Hard when it took significant effort. - Use Good as default success. - Use Easy sparingly but not never. This alone often cuts review load because you stop resetting cards unnecessarily.
I’m also confused about “ease” and why some cards become “low ease” and then I see them constantly.

Ease (often shown as a percentage, like 250%) is Anki’s estimate of how easy the card is for you. When you press Again or Hard, ease can drop (depending on algorithm/version). Lower ease means future intervals grow more slowly, so the card comes back more often. If you frequently fail a card, its ease can sink, and it becomes a “vampire” that demands constant attention. How to prevent ease collapse: - Don’t press Again for minor imperfections. - Fix the card if it’s repeatedly difficult. - Avoid ambiguous prompts and overly broad answers. How to recover: - Editing the card helps more than trying to “grind” it. - Some people use “ease reset” tools, but that’s a band-aid. Better to fix the underlying problem. Also, if you’re using the newer scheduling algorithm (FSRS), ease works differently. But regardless, repeated failures increase workload.
I’ve heard of FSRS. Should I switch?

Not yet. FSRS can be great, but it’s another variable. First stabilize with good cards and sustainable intake. Then, if you’re curious, you can switch and tune it with your review history. If you switch while overloaded and inconsistent, you may misinterpret the results. So: stick to default scheduling for a month while you fix card design and habits. After that, FSRS might reduce workload by better fitting your memory patterns, but it’s optional.
Okay. Can we do a practical exercise? Like take my broad card and turn it into a set of good cards.

Yes. Let’s do it methodically. Take your example: Front: “What are the main points of [concept]?” Back: 8 bullet points. We’ll turn it into: 1) One “core” card Front: “What is the central idea of [concept]?” Back: “One-sentence paraphrase.” 2) One “purpose” card Front: “What problem does [concept] address?” Back: “One sentence.” 3) Two or three “key distinctions” cards Front: “In [concept], what’s the difference between A and B?” (if applicable) Back: “A = …; B = …” 4) Atomic bullet cards For each of the 8 bullets, decide: - Is it a key rule/definition? Make a short Q/A. - Is it a detail/example? Put in Extra or make an application card. Example structure for one bullet: Front: “In [concept], what does ‘X’ mean?” Back: “X = … (simple).” Extra: “Long explanation and context.” If a bullet is itself a list, it must be split again. Now: you don’t need to keep all 8 as separate cards if some are low value. You might keep 4 and archive the rest. If you paste one anonymized bullet list (just the structure, not the subject), I can show exactly how I’d split it.
I can’t paste it because it would reveal the topic. But imagine it’s like: definition, purpose, 3 subtypes, steps, common mistakes, and an example.

That’s enough structure to work with. Here’s how I’d convert that structure: A) Definition card Front: “Define [concept] (one sentence).” Back: “…” B) Purpose card Front: “What is [concept] used for?” Back: “…” C) Subtypes If there are 3 subtypes: - Card 1: “What are the 3 subtypes of [concept]?” Back: “Subtype A, B, C.” - Cards 2–4: one per subtype: Front: “What distinguishes subtype A?” Back: “Key distinguishing feature (1–2 bullets).” If the subtype differences are subtle, add: - “Subtype A vs B: key difference?” card. D) Steps/procedure If steps are important: - Overview card: “What are the 3 steps of [process]?” (only if truly 3–5) - Then one card per step: “In step 1, what do you do?” If there are many steps (8+), consider whether you need Anki for the whole procedure. Sometimes it’s better to practice the procedure outside Anki and only Anki the “decision points” and common errors. E) Common mistakes Make 1–2 cards: - “What is a common mistake when doing [concept]?” - “How do you avoid mistake X?” Keep it to the top 2–3 mistakes. Don’t Anki every possible mistake. F) Example Make an application card: Front: “Given [short example], which subtype/step applies?” Back: “It’s subtype B because …” Or: Front: “Is this a correct application? Why/why not?” Back: “No, because it violates rule X.” That set might be ~8–12 cards, but each is answerable quickly.
That seems doable. But I’m worried about making too many cards for one thing.

The number of cards should match how often you need to retrieve the info and how complex it is. But there’s a constraint: your daily time budget. So you can cap card creation: - For any single concept, limit yourself to (say) 10 cards max. If you feel you need 30, that’s a signal you’re trying to store a whole chapter. Better to store the skeleton and keep the rest in notes. Also, you can delay detail: - Start with 3–5 cards. - If you later notice a gap (you keep forgetting a nuance), add one more card then. This “just-in-time” expansion prevents overbuilding.
I think I overbuilt everything upfront.

Most people do. The fix is to shift to incremental expansion: - Start small. - Let your real difficulties guide what you add. This is similar to product development: ship a minimal version, then iterate based on actual failure modes.
Can we talk about review speed? I’m slow. I read everything, I hesitate, I get distracted.

Speed comes from (1) card design, (2) decision rules, and (3) environment. 1) Card design Short answers, clear prompts, fewer gray areas. This is the biggest. 2) Decision rules - 10-second rule: if you can’t answer in ~10 seconds, flip, mark Again/Hard, and move on. Don’t sit there suffering; that trains procrastination. - Grade quickly. Don’t overthink. 3) Environment - Turn on airplane mode or focus mode during reviews. - Use a timer. - Do reviews in a consistent place/time if possible. Also, consider turning off “show answer timer” if it stresses you, but some people like it as feedback. If you find yourself rereading the card multiple times, it’s often because the question is vague. Rewrite it.
I definitely reread. My prompts are vague like “Explain why…” or “Describe…”

Vague verbs are trouble. Replace them with specific outputs: - “Give 2 reasons why…” - “State the rule that…” - “What is the consequence of…” - “What is the first step when…” - “Which of these applies…” Also, add constraints to “why” questions: Bad: “Why does X happen?” Better: “What is the main mechanism behind X? (1 sentence)” Or: “Name 2 factors that contribute to X.” This makes grading easy. If you keep “Describe…”, you’ll never know when you’ve “described enough,” so you’ll keep pressing Again.
How do I handle stuff that’s inherently long, like a process with many steps?

Three approaches: 1) Chunk the process Group steps into 3–5 phases. Make cards for: - Phase names/order - Key decision points per phase - Common errors per phase 2) Focus on triggers and decisions Instead of memorizing every step, memorize: - “When do you do step X?” - “What indicates you should move to next phase?” - “What’s the most common failure point?” 3) Use Anki for anchors, practice for fluency Use Anki to remember the outline and key constraints, and then practice executing the process outside Anki (writing it out, doing it, simulating). Anki maintains the map; practice builds the route. If you try to Anki every micro-step, you’ll get crushed.
That makes sense. I think I was using Anki as the only learning method.

Exactly. Anki is a scheduler for retrieval practice, not a complete learning system. A balanced system: - Initial understanding: reading, watching, working through examples. - Consolidation: paraphrase, make a few cards. - Maintenance: Anki. - Integration: occasional synthesis (write a summary, solve a problem, teach it). If you skip understanding and jump straight to card creation, you get brittle memories and high failure rates.
I also make cards while I’m still confused, hoping Anki will make me learn it.

Anki won’t fix confusion; it will just schedule confusion repeatedly. Rule: only make cards for things you can explain simply right now. If you can’t explain it: - Put it in Inbox as “needs understanding.” - Later, once you understand, convert to cards. If you must make a card while confused, make a “pointer” card: Front: “Look up X / clarify Y” Back: link/reference But don’t keep many pointer cards long-term; they’re basically tasks, not memory.
I have tons of pointer cards. Like “Read about X” and then I feel guilty when it comes up.

Those are productivity tasks disguised as flashcards. They belong in a task manager or notes, not Anki. I recommend: - Search for cards that start with “Read” / “Look up” / “Research”. - Suspend them or move them to a separate deck that you don’t review daily. - Convert the important ones into actual questions once you’ve learned them. This will immediately reduce guilt and review load.
Wow. I didn’t realize I was doing that. I basically turned Anki into a guilt machine.
Many people do. The fix is to separate: - Memory practice (Anki) - Work planning (to-do list) - Reference (notes/wiki) Once those roles are separated, each system becomes lighter and more effective.
Okay. If I do all this, how long until my reviews calm down?

You should feel relief within 1–2 weeks if you: - Set New/day = 0 - Stop using Again for wording perfection - Adjust learning steps to reduce churn (optional) - Begin suspending reverse cards and pointer cards - Start editing the worst offenders The review count may not drop instantly because due cards are already scheduled, but your per-review time and failure rate should improve quickly, which makes the pile feel manageable. Then over 3–6 weeks, as you stop feeding new cards and fix leeches, the daily due count usually decreases substantially. If you keep adding new cards at high volume, it won’t calm down.
I’m tempted to just delete the deck and start over.

Starting over feels clean, but it often recreates the same habits. Better: keep the deck, change the process. However, a partial reset can help psychologically: - Suspend a large subset of problematic cards (like all reverse, all pointer, all broad-summary). - Then rebuild gradually from the remaining stable core. You can also export the deck as a backup, then do aggressive suspension without fear. If you still want a “fresh start,” do it as: - New deck with strict rules and low new/day. - Old deck archived, not deleted.
I like the “archive old deck” idea. But then I have two piles.

Only if you review both. If the archived deck is suspended or in a profile you don’t open daily, it won’t create workload. Anki lets you keep decks without studying them. The key is to ensure they don’t have active due cards you feel obligated to do. You can: - Suspend all cards in the archive deck. - Or set its new/day and reviews/day to 0. Suspension is more definitive.
I’m going to do that. But I still need a plan for tomorrow. What should I do first?

Tomorrow plan (45 minutes max): 1) 5 minutes: Set New/day = 0 in your main deck options. 2) 30 minutes: Do reviews with a timer. Use the grading rule: core idea remembered = Good, not Again. 3) 10 minutes: Flag/tag the worst 10 cards you encounter (broad, confusing, long, repeated failures). Don’t edit yet. Then, later this week on desktop: - Open Browser, sort by Lapses/Ease, tag 30 worst cards. - Suspend pointer cards. - Suspend reverse cards in bulk if you can identify them. If you can only do one desktop action: suspend reverse cards. That’s the fastest workload cut.
How do I bulk suspend reverse cards? I’m scared I’ll suspend the wrong ones.

On desktop, in Browse: 1) Select your deck. 2) In the search bar, you can filter by note type if you used “Basic (and reversed card)”. For example: note:"Basic (and reversed card)" 3) In the card list, you’ll see each note has Card 1 and Card 2. 4) Sort by “Card” column, or select Card 2 entries. This can be tricky depending on view, but often you can: - Search for "card:2" within that note type (some versions support card:2). If not: - Click a card, look at the right panel; it shows “Card 1 of 2” etc. You can select all, then use “Cards…” menu to choose which card template you’re viewing. If this feels too fiddly, do a safer method: - Don’t bulk suspend immediately. - Instead, as you review, if you notice a reverse card that feels pointless, suspend it right then (there’s usually a suspend action in the menu). This is slower but safer. If you tell me whether your reverse cards are from a specific note type, I can give a more exact search.
I think they’re from “Basic (and reversed card)”. I can try the note search.

Great. Do a small pilot: - Filter note:"Basic (and reversed card)" - Pick 20 notes. - Suspend only Card 2 for those. Then watch your workload for a few days. If nothing bad happens (it won’t), expand. Also, you can always unsuspend if needed.
Okay. Another thing: sometimes I make cards with multiple clozes in one note, like c1, c2, c3. Is that basically like 3 cards?

Yes. Each cloze deletion generates a separate card (unless you specifically configure otherwise). So one note with c1–c3 is three cards. If you have lots of notes with 5–10 clozes, your card count and review load are much larger than you think. Also, if those clozes are in one paragraph, you might be learning the paragraph rather than the facts. A cleanup strategy: - In Browser, search for notes with many clozes (hard to search directly, but you can inspect and tag). - For high-cloze notes, consider rewriting into fewer, clearer cards. For example: Instead of clozing 6 terms in a paragraph, create: - one card for the core idea, - a couple cards for key distinctions, - and maybe 1–2 for important terms.
I think I made some notes with like 10 clozes. No wonder.

Exactly. That alone can explain the avalanche. If you want a fast fix: suspend some of those cloze cards temporarily. - You can suspend individual cards generated from the note without deleting the note. Then later you can decide which deletions are worth keeping. A good rule: one cloze deletion per note, unless you have a strong reason.
But making separate notes is slower.

Slightly, but it improves quality and reduces future time. Also, you can still be fast by using a consistent pattern: - One sentence, one deletion. - Duplicate the note if needed and change the deletion. But again, don’t feel obligated to keep every deletion. Most of the time, one well-chosen deletion captures the key memory.
What about “incremental reading” workflows? People say you can automatically make cards from highlights.

Those workflows can be powerful but are risky for exactly your problem: they generate lots of low-processed cards. If you’re already drowning, automation will amplify it. If you use highlighting tools: - Use them only to capture raw material into an inbox. - Do not auto-convert highlights into cards without rewriting. The bottleneck should be your understanding and paraphrasing, not your ability to generate cards.
I feel like I’m losing time because I’m not “covering” everything.

You’re gaining time because you’re building a system you can actually sustain. “Covering everything” is an illusion if it leads to burnout and abandonment. A sustainable pace beats an unsustainable sprint. Also, your brain benefits from depth over breadth. Fewer, better cards create stronger mental models than thousands of shallow clozes.
Okay. Can we talk about metrics? How do I know if I’m improving?

Track a few simple metrics weekly (not daily, to avoid obsession): 1) Daily review time Are you consistently within 30–45 minutes? 2) Reviews/day Is the number stable or decreasing? 3) Again rate Roughly: how often are you pressing Again? If it’s >20–25% on mature cards, card design/grading may still be too strict. 4) Leech count Are you generating many leeches? If yes, you’re still making too-hard cards. 5) Deck health Do you dread opening Anki less? That’s a real metric. Anki has stats that show review counts and answer buttons. Use them as feedback, not as a scoreboard.
I tend to obsess over stats. Then I tweak settings and it gets worse.

Then set a “no tweaks” rule: - Only review stats once per week. - Only allow settings changes once per month. - Spend that energy on card editing instead. Most improvements come from better prompts and answers, not algorithm tweaks.
Okay. I’m going to try. But I’m still overwhelmed by the sheer pile right now.

Then let’s reduce the pile quickly with three high-impact actions: 1) Set New/day = 0 immediately. 2) Suspend obvious non-memory cards (pointer cards). 3) Suspend reverse cards in bulk (at least for a large subset). If you do those, your due count may still be high for a bit, but it will stop growing and will start shrinking. Also, allow yourself to not finish all reviews for a few days. Do your timed 30–45 minutes and stop. Consistency will chip away at it. If you try to “clear the pile” in one marathon, you’ll associate Anki with suffering and avoid it.
When I don’t finish, the number tomorrow is even bigger and I panic.

That’s why we pause new cards and reduce future workload. In the short term, yes, tomorrow may be bigger. But if you can’t finish today anyway, panicking won’t help. A trick: set a “maximum due” goal rather than “zero due.” Example: “I will keep due under 250.” If it’s 400 today, you do 45 minutes, maybe it drops to 320. That’s progress. Also, you can prioritize: - Do reviews due today first. - Leave learning cards for later if they churn. But typically Anki handles ordering; you can also use filtered decks for “overdue first” if needed.
I wish I could just pause the scheduling until I’m ready.

You can approximate that by: - Taking a short break (1–2 days) if you’re burned out. But longer breaks increase backlog. Better: keep a tiny daily habit even during chaos: - 10 minutes per day minimum. This keeps the system from exploding and maintains momentum. If you truly need a pause, consider “vacation mode” by setting a review limit and doing a small number daily. But again, the key is to stop new cards and fix leeches.
I did take a break for a week once and it was a disaster.
Exactly. Anki punishes long breaks because due dates accumulate. That’s why a small daily minimum is so important. Think of it like brushing teeth: not intense, just consistent.
Okay. One more thing: I sometimes do reviews and realize I don’t care about the info anymore, but I keep it because it’s in the deck.

That’s a strong signal to suspend. Interests and priorities change; your deck should evolve. Add a “still relevant?” check: - If a card feels irrelevant 3 times in a row, suspend it. This keeps your deck aligned with current goals and reduces workload. Also, your deck should not be a museum of everything you once thought mattered.
I’m going to start suspending more aggressively. But I’m afraid I’ll suspend something important accidentally.

Use tags to make it reversible: - Tag cards “suspended_relevance” before suspending. Then you can review that tag monthly and unsuspend anything you regret. This reduces fear and allows experimentation.
Okay. I think I have a plan now. But can you summarize the exact steps I should take this week?

This week plan: Daily (30–45 minutes): 1) Reviews only (New/day = 0). 2) Use grading rule: only Again on true failure; don’t punish wording. 3) Flag/tag any card that is broad, ambiguous, too long, or repeatedly failed. Twice this week (30 minutes each, desktop): 1) Browse → sort by Lapses or Ease. 2) Tag top 30 problem cards “triage”. 3) For each triage card: - Split into smaller cards OR rewrite prompt/answer to be short and clear. - Move long text to Extra (or below an “Extra” separator). 4) Find and suspend: - Pointer/task cards (“Read/Look up…”) - Reverse cards (Card 2) unless truly needed - Any card you don’t care about anymore Optional (once, if relearning loops are awful): - Change learning steps to 15m 1d. Next week: - If daily reviews are manageable, reintroduce 5 new/day from a curated inbox. If you follow this, you should feel significantly less overwhelmed within 1–2 weeks.
Thanks. I’m going to do it. But I know myself: I’ll start strong then slip. How do I keep it going?

Build guardrails: 1) Set a fixed daily appointment Same time each day (e.g., right after work). Treat it like a meeting. 2) Use a timer every time No timer = drift into misery. Timer = predictable. 3) Make it easy to start Keep Anki on your home screen, notifications off, and your environment ready. 4) Define “minimum” Even on bad days: 10 minutes. This prevents backlog explosions. 5) Weekly maintenance slot Put two 30-minute “deck cleanup” sessions on your calendar. Without maintenance, decks degrade. 6) Don’t increase new cards when you feel motivated Motivation spikes are dangerous. Increase only when stats/time show stability for at least a week. 7) Keep an inbox So you can capture without committing. If you want, we can craft a “personal Anki constitution” of 10 rules you agree to follow. That can reduce the urge to improvise.
Yes, give me the 10 rules. I need something to follow when I’m panicking.

Here are 10 rules to keep your system stable: 1) Reviews come first. No new cards until due reviews are done (or until you hit your time cap). 2) New cards are a subscription. Add only what you’re willing to review for years. 3) Default new/day is low (5–10). Increase only after 7 stable days. 4) Again is for true failure only. Imperfect recall is Hard/Good. 5) If a card takes >15 seconds to answer, rewrite or split it. 6) Long explanations go in Extra, not in the required answer. 7) Broad prompts (“main points,” “describe,” “explain everything”) are banned. 8) Reverse cards are opt-in, not default. 9) Leeches are design problems. Edit or suspend; don’t grind. 10) Use an inbox for capture; process it on schedule. Print these somewhere or keep them as a pinned note. When you panic, follow the rules instead of your feelings.
I’m going to save that. One last question: when I split a card, do I delete the old one?

Usually: - Create the new smaller cards first. - Then either suspend the old broad card or rewrite it into an overview card. I recommend suspending the old one rather than deleting, at least initially. Tag it “replaced” so you know it’s obsolete. Sometimes keeping one overview card is useful, but only if it’s answerable quickly (e.g., “Name the 3 categories…” not “list 8 points with explanations”).
Got it. I’ll start tomorrow with New=0 and a timer. If I get stuck again, I’ll ask.
Does the Pomodoro technique actually work, or is it just popular? What does the research say about timed work intervals?

The Pomodoro technique (classically 25 minutes of focused work + 5-minute break, repeated, with a longer break after four cycles) is popular, but the scientific evidence is more indirect than “Pomodoro was tested in a large randomized trial and won.” Research tends to support the underlying components: (1) working in bounded intervals, (2) taking brief breaks, (3) using goals and feedback, and (4) managing cognitive fatigue and vigilance. The precise 25/5 split is more tradition than empirically optimized. What research does say, in a nutshell: - Vigilance and sustained performance often decline over time on a single task, and brief breaks can restore performance, especially for attention-demanding tasks. - Spacing work with breaks can reduce mental fatigue and mind-wandering and can improve accuracy and persistence on some tasks. - Timeboxing (committing to work for a fixed period) can reduce “starting friction” and can encourage better planning and monitoring. - Shorter blocks can help with tasks that have uncertain scope by forcing regular checkpoints. But results vary by task type (deep reading vs. routine processing), by individual differences (baseline endurance, preference for switching), and by how “breaks” are used (true disengagement vs. doing another demanding task). If you tell me what kind of work you’re trying to optimize (writing, coding, studying, design, admin), I can map the evidence to a more tailored interval strategy.

I mainly do writing and analysis: drafting reports, synthesizing sources, building arguments. Sometimes I also do spreadsheet work and email, but the core is writing. I’m not looking for motivational hacks; I want something evidence-based.

For writing + analysis, the most evidence-aligned way to use Pomodoro is to treat it as a structured self-regulation tool rather than a magical ratio. Key evidence-relevant mechanisms for your work: 1) Cognitive fatigue and vigilance decrement Sustained, demanding cognitive work tends to show performance decrements over time: slower processing, more errors, reduced monitoring. Short breaks can restore aspects of performance, especially when they involve true disengagement. For writing, the “errors” may be weaker coherence, missed contradictions, or drifting structure. Practical implication: breaks aren’t optional “rewards”; they’re part of maintaining quality control. 2) Goal-gradient + implementation intentions Timeboxing creates a near-term goal (“work until the timer ends”) which can increase persistence and reduce the tendency to over-polish early. It’s similar to an implementation intention: “For the next 25 minutes I will only draft section X.” This is supported by research on specific plans improving follow-through. Practical implication: in each interval, define a concrete deliverable (e.g., “draft two paragraphs answering claim A with citations 1–3”) rather than “work on report.” 3) Spacing and incubation For complex writing, stepping away can improve insight and restructuring. There’s evidence for incubation effects—breaks can help solve problems, particularly when you genuinely shift away and then return. Practical implication: put harder conceptual moves (argument architecture, headings, thesis refinement) near the start of a block, then use breaks to let it incubate. 4) Metacognitive calibration Regular checkpoints (every 25–50 minutes) force you to ask: “Is this the right direction?” That reduces sunk-cost drift. Practical implication: use the end of each interval for a 30–60 second log: what changed in the draft, next micro-goal, any open questions. On the “25/5” specifics: Many people doing writing find 45/10, 50/10, or 60/10 produces better continuity. Evidence doesn’t crown one; it’s a trade-off between maintaining immersion vs. preventing drift/fatigue. If you want to be evidence-seeking, I’d recommend an A/B approach: pick two interval schemes for two weeks each and track objective outputs (words drafted, sections completed, revision passes) and quality proxies (number of structural changes needed later, coherence rating, edit density).
What does “brief breaks restore performance” mean in actual studies? Like what’s measured, and how strong is the effect?

In lab and applied studies, “restore performance” is typically operationalized as improvements relative to the end of a continuous work period. Common measures include: - Reaction time and accuracy on sustained attention tasks (e.g., go/no-go, psychomotor vigilance tasks). - Error rates on repetitive but attention-demanding tasks (e.g., proofreading, data entry). - Speed/accuracy trade-offs in problem-solving or working-memory tasks. - Self-reported fatigue, effort, and mind-wandering frequency. - In some applied settings: productivity metrics (units processed), quality metrics (defects), or time-on-task. Effect strength varies widely because: - Break duration differs (microbreaks of 30 seconds vs. 5–10 minutes). - Break activity differs (rest, walking, stretching, phone use, another cognitive task). - Task differs (monotonous vigilance vs. creative generation). - Baseline fatigue differs (time of day, prior workload). Patterns that are fairly consistent: - Performance tends to decline during long, continuous vigilance tasks; short breaks can reset or partially restore performance. - Microbreaks (even 30–120 seconds) can help in repetitive tasks, especially when they include physical movement or gaze shifts. - Breaks that are cognitively demanding (e.g., reading dense material, intense messaging) often restore less than breaks that involve rest or light movement. For writing, the mapping is indirect. Writing isn’t a simple reaction time task, but it has a vigilance component (monitoring coherence, catching contradictions) and an executive component (maintaining plan, inhibiting tangents). Breaks can help those executive functions recover. If you want something closer to writing: studies on proofreading and editing show that accuracy can drop with continuous time and can improve after breaks. Also, research on “interleaving” and spacing in learning suggests that distributed practice (with breaks) improves later recall and transfer—relevant if your writing depends on synthesizing sources.
I’ve heard some people say breaks interrupt flow and reduce quality. Is that supported?

It’s plausible and sometimes observed, depending on how “flow” is defined and how interruptions are implemented. What’s supported: - Context switching has costs. When you stop mid-thought and switch tasks, resumption can take time, and you can lose the mental state (what to do next, why you chose a structure). - External interruptions (unplanned, unpredictable) are generally more disruptive than planned breaks because they occur at arbitrary points and can create stress or frustration. What’s more nuanced: - Planned breaks can be less disruptive if they occur at natural boundaries (end of a paragraph, finishing a sub-argument, completing a table). - Longer blocks can support deeper immersion for tasks requiring complex integration, like writing an argument. Many writers do better with 45–90 minutes than 25 minutes. So the evidence doesn’t say “breaks always help” or “breaks always hurt.” It says: - Breaks can restore attention and reduce fatigue, - But interruptions can harm continuity, - Therefore, the optimal strategy is often: fewer, well-timed breaks aligned with task boundaries. A practical evidence-aligned modification is “boundary-based Pomodoro”: - Work until you reach a meaningful boundary (finish a section, solve a subproblem), with an upper cap (say 60–75 minutes). - Then take a break (5–10 minutes). This keeps the benefits of spacing while reducing the cost of cutting off a complex cognitive chunk.
If I want to test this properly for myself, what would a good experiment look like? I don’t want vague journaling.

A solid self-experiment can be structured like a small N-of-1 trial with clear metrics and counterbalancing. Here’s a concrete design for writing/analysis: 1) Choose conditions (2–3) Example: A) 25/5 (classic) B) 50/10 (longer immersion) C) 90/15 (deep work style) 2) Define tasks and keep them comparable Pick a project type that repeats: weekly report, literature synthesis, policy memo. Try to keep task difficulty similar across days, or at least log difficulty. 3) Define objective outcome metrics For writing, consider: - Draft output: words drafted in “draft mode” (not counting rewriting). - Structural progress: number of sections completed to a “good enough” draft state. - Citation integration: number of sources incorporated with correct attribution. - Revision load: later edit distance (e.g., track changes count) or time spent revising per 1000 words. - Quality proxy: after 24 hours, rate coherence on a rubric (thesis clarity, logical flow, evidence alignment) blind to condition if possible (e.g., hide timestamps and have a colleague rate, if feasible). 4) Define process metrics - Start latency: minutes from sitting down to first meaningful sentence. - Break compliance: did you actually take breaks as planned? - Break activity: rest/walk vs. screens. - Resumption time: time to regain momentum after break (you can measure by time until next new paragraph). 5) Use a schedule that controls for day effects A simple approach: randomized blocks. - Week 1: ABABA (alternate days) - Week 2: BABAB Or randomize each day with constraints. 6) Set a minimum sample size At least 8–10 sessions per condition. For writing, variability is high, so fewer than that can be misleading. 7) Analyze with simple statistics - Compare means and variability for output and revision load. - Plot resumption time vs. interval length. - Look for trade-offs: maybe 90/15 yields fewer words but lower revision load. If you tell me your typical weekly cadence (how many focused sessions you can run), I can propose a schedule that fits.
I can do about 2 focused sessions per day, 5 days a week. Also, what should I do during breaks if the goal is restoration? I don’t want to just scroll.

With 2 sessions/day × 5 days/week, you can get 10 sessions/week. Over 3 weeks, that’s 30 sessions—enough to compare 3 conditions with ~10 sessions each. Break activity: the evidence generally favors “low cognitive load + some physical disengagement.” Options that tend to be restorative: - Stand up, walk briefly, change posture. - Look at far distance (reduce visual fatigue if you’re screen-heavy). - Light stretching. - Hydration, quick snack if needed. - Brief breathing exercise (not mystical—just downshifts arousal and can reset). Things that often restore less: - Consuming dense information (news, technical reading). - Rapid-fire messaging that creates new cognitive threads. - Starting a new task that leaves residue (you return thinking about it). A practical break protocol: - 1–2 minutes: stand, move, look away from screen. - Remaining time: “idle” or very light activity (tidy desk, refill water). - Last 10–20 seconds: write a resumption cue on a sticky note: “Next: write counterargument paragraph; cite Smith 2021; end with transition.” That resumption cue is a strong way to reduce restart costs.
You mentioned “cognitive residue.” Is that a real concept in research?

Yes, often discussed as “attention residue” or “task switching costs.” The core idea: when you switch away from a task, part of your attention remains allocated to the previous task, reducing performance on the new task (and potentially vice versa when you come back). It’s been studied in multitasking and task switching research. What’s robust: - Switching tasks generally incurs a measurable cost: slower responses and more errors. - Even after switching, performance can remain impaired for a short period because the previous task set remains partially active. - The cost is larger when tasks are complex or when the prior task is unfinished. Applied implication for breaks: - If your break involves starting another cognitively engaging activity (like reading something argumentative), you can carry residue back into writing. - A “clean break” (movement, rest) tends to minimize residue. For writing, the practical translation is: avoid breaks that create new open loops.
Ok. Another question: is 25 minutes too short for writing? It often takes me 10 minutes just to warm up.

For many writing tasks, 25 minutes can be short, especially if you’re doing high-level synthesis and argumentation.
